data.title: U.S. stocks continue to drop as inflation fears take center stage
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages slid on Thursday as investors assessed more economic data and digested the Federal Reserve's decision to keep interest rates unchanged.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last -0.6%, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -0.7%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was -0.8%.

Over in the bond market, the benchmark 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 1 basis point higher at 4.28%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) jumped 6 basis points to 3.84%, after reaching a seven-month high.

“Today is the first day I sense a bit of panic in the air,” said Helene Meisler, market technician and equity trader.

On Wednesday, the Fed issued its decision to keep interest rates unchanged within a range of 3.5%-3.75%, in line with expectations.

Fed Chair Jerome Powell said it is important to keep interest rates mildly restrictive, and the Fed added a new sentence to its guidance stating that the economic implications of the conflict in the Middle East were “uncertain.” 

“Despite the continued escalation in the Middle East War—particularly the latest attacks on energy infrastructure by both Iran and Israel—quite a few market participants are holding on to the view that the global economy is still in a ‘mean reversion’ paradigm: the view that the genie of energy damage and economic financial disruption can be put quickly back in the lamp,” said Mohamed El-Erian, chief economic adviser at Allianz.

“What is in play here is not a narrow ‘transitory’ dynamic when it comes to the disruptive economic and financial effects. We are more in the world of ‘multiple equilibrium,’ with each tipping point fueling this dynamic,” he said.

Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) were higher at $97.4, while Brent futures (CO1:COM) were at $112.5 per barrel.

In economic news, the Philly Fed Manufacturing Index unexpectedly rose in March, while the initial jobless claims unexpectedly dropped in the past week.

Lastly, new home sales slumped -17.6% MoM in January to 587K vs. 728K consensus, according to the U.S. Census Bureau.

data.title: Investor sentiment weakens as bearish outlook rises, shows latest AAII survey
data.description:
Market sentiment among individual investors grew more pessimistic, according to the latest survey from the American Association of Individual Investors.

Bullish sentiment, or expectations that stock prices will rise over the next six months, slipped to 30.4% for the week ended March 18, down from 31.9% in the prior week, while bearish sentiment surged to 52.0% from 46.4% a week earlier.

Equity markets remained under pressure through the week, weighed down by a combination of rising geopolitical tensions and persistent inflation concerns. Escalating risks in the Middle East, including attacks on energy infrastructure and fears of supply disruptions, pushed oil prices sharply higher, with crude hovering near the $100 per barrel mark and raising concerns about higher fuel costs.

The surge in energy prices added to inflation worries, while recent data, including a hotter-than-expected producer price index, reinforced expectations that interest rates could remain elevated for longer.

At the same time, Treasury yields climbed across the curve, with the 2-year yield hitting a multi-month high as markets scaled back hopes for near-term rate cuts following the Federal Reserve’s decision to hold rates steady. The shift in rate expectations, alongside elevated inflation risks, pressured equities, with the S&P 500 breaking below its 200-day moving average and touching a four-month low.

Volatility remained elevated, and broader risk sentiment weakened, as investors navigated the combined impact of geopolitical uncertainty, tighter financial conditions, and growing concerns about the economic outlook.

According to AAII, neutral sentiment, or expectations that stock prices will stay essentially unchanged over the next six months, also declined during the week, with the figure falling to 17.6%, compared to 21.7% last week.

The survey has been conducted by the American Association of Individual Investors since 1987, in which it asks respondents for their thoughts on where the market is heading in the next six months.

 

S&P 500 Tracking Funds: (MUTF:FXAIX), (MUTF:VFIAX), (MUTF:VFFSX), (MUTF:SWPPX), (NYSEARCA:SPY), (NYSEARCA:VOO), (NYSEARCA:IVV), (NYSEARCA:RSP), (NYSEARCA:SSO), (NYSEARCA:SH), (NYSEARCA:SDS), and (NYSEARCA:SPXU).

data.title: Just 4 homebuilders hold strong profit grades as housing market slumps
data.description:
The housing market is facing significant headwinds, with new home sales falling to their lowest level since October 2022. Amid the challenging environment, only four U.S. homebuilder stocks have managed to maintain strong profitability grades.

Seeking Alpha's stock screener filtered for U.S.-based homebuilding companies trading on primary exchanges with strong profitability ratings. Specifically, the criteria selected stocks in the homebuilding sector that carry A+, A or A- profitability grades, indicating robust financial performance and margin strength relative to industry peers.

Now, here is the list: 

  1. NVR, Inc. (NVR) - Market Cap: $18.1B | Profitability Grade: A 

  2. Lennar Corporation (LEN) - Market Cap: $23.3B | Profitability Grade: A

  3. D.R. Horton, Inc. (DHI) - Market Cap: $39.8B | Profitability Grade: A- |

  4. PulteGroup, Inc. (PHM) - Market Cap: $22.4B | Profitability Grade: A-

The timing of this screen is particularly relevant given the deteriorating housing market conditions. New home sales slumped 17.6% month-over-month to 587K in January, significantly missing the 728K consensus estimate. This marks the weakest reading in more than three years, while the median sales price of new houses sold dropped to $400.5K, down 6.8% from the year-ago price.

Housing inventory has also risen to 9.7 months of supply at the current sales rate, up 21.3% from the prior month. These headwinds make profitability grades increasingly important for investors seeking homebuilders positioned to weather the downturn.

“We continue to hope that the spring housing market will materialize in something more upbeat, but January’s new home sales represent a significant setback," said Mark Hamrick, senior economic analyst at Bankrate.

Real Estate ETFs: (VNQ), (XLRE), (IYR), (USRT), and (HOMZ).

data.title: OpenAI secures HBM4 supply from Samsung to build its first AI chip, Titan: report
data.description:
Samsung (SSNLF) appears to be the provider of memory for OpenAI's (OPENAI) in-house artificial intelligence chip Titan, according to Business Korea.

On Thursday, Samsung agreed to exclusively supply OpenAI with up to 800M gigabits of HBM4 during the second half of 2026, the report said, citing industry insiders. This would account for 15% of Samsung's expected HBM4 production in 2026 and be its largest deal behind those made with Nvidia (NVDA) and AMD (AMD).  

OpenAI is collaborating with Broadcom (AVGO) on its in-house AI chip. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) is expected to begin mass production of Titan during the second half of 2026. It has a tentative release date of late 2026.

Until recently, OpenAI had solely relied on Nvidia's GPUs to provide the computing power needed for its AI models. However, in October 2025, it entered into a multi-year deal with AMD to deploy 6 gigawatts of AMD's GPUs. The first gigawatt deployment of AMD Instinct MI450 GPUs is set to start in the second half of 2026.

data.title: Oil rallies, but some analysts suggest the idea of $150-$200 a barrel is a stretch
data.description:
Brent crude surged to roughly $110 per barrel as escalating tensions in the Persian Gulf intensified concerns over global supply. Notably, the rally began even before hostilities formally erupted on February 28, with markets embedding a geopolitical risk premium weeks in advance.

In total, prices have climbed about 60% since the onset of the crisis, reflecting mounting fears of disruption through the critical Strait of Hormuz, Robin Brooks, senior fellow at the Brookings Institution, said. 

Analysis of current pricing suggests that oil markets are already factoring in a severe supply shock. Brooks noted that using standard elasticity-based models, the present price level implies that traders are discounting a scenario in which roughly half of Persian Gulf oil exports are permanently curtailed. Recent export flows—led by Saudi Arabia and Iran—indicate volumes nearing 10M barrels per day, or about half of pre-conflict throughput. 

Despite widespread speculation that oil could spike to $150 or even $200, such outcomes would likely require significantly more extreme disruptions than those currently implied, Brooks added in a Substack post. Elasticity estimates suggest that even under severe constraints, price increases of that magnitude are unlikely.

Importantly, downside risks remain. A potential easing of conflict, shifting geopolitical strategies, or policy interventions—such as tighter sanctions on Iranian exports—could alleviate supply concerns. As uncertainty persists, markets remain highly sensitive to both escalation and resolution scenarios.

Oil ETFs: (USO), (UCO), (DBO), (OILK), and (USL). 

Natural Gas ETFs: (UNG), (BOIL), and (UNL).

Energy ETFs: (XLE), (VDE), (XOP), (OIH), (AMLP), and (IXC).

data.title: Goldman Sachs, JPMorgan, BofA offer products that let hedge funds bet against private credit market - report
data.description:
Goldman Sachs Group (GS), JPMorgan Chase (JPM), and Bank of America (BAC) are said to be among the investment banks that offer hedge funds a way to bet against the private credit market.

Private credit is seeing an ongoing crisis, with several companies curbing withdrawals from their private credit funds.

The investment banks have assembled baskets of listed companies with exposure to the $1.8 trillion private credit market, people with knowledge of the matter told Bloomberg News.

Goldman has indexes that focus on European financial institutions with private credit exposure, a group of business development companies, and alternative managers. JPMorgan's basket includes alternative managers and BDCs, according to the people who did not wish to be identified discussing bespoke product offerings.

Bank of America reportedly had a basket of European financial firms with exposure to private credit.

data.title: GSK granted FDA nod for Lynavoy for itch associated with rare liver disorder
data.description:
GSK (GSK) announced on Thursday that the U.S. FDA approved Lynavoy as a treatment for an itching condition called cholestatic pruritus associated with primary biliary cholangitis ((PBC)), a rare disorder characterized by disruption to bile flow from the liver.

Indicated for adults with cholestatic pruritus due to PBC, Lynavoy, also known as linerixibat, is the first medicine approved in the U.S. for the condition, the British drugmaker said.

GSK (GSK) has signed an agreement to manufacture and market linerixibat globally in collaboration with Alfasigma, an Italian pharma company that acquired U.S. liver drugmaker Intercept Pharmaceuticals in 2023.

The approval is backed by data from the company’s GLISTEN Phase 3 trial, which met primary and key secondary endpoints as Lynavoy led to a sustained reduction in cholestatic pruritus and itch-related sleep disturbances over 24 weeks.

“This is the first liver medicine from our pipeline to receive approval, underscoring our commitment to developing meaningful innovation across the spectrum of liver disease,” said Kaivan Khavandi, GSK’s (GSK) R&D head for Respiratory, Immunology & Inflammation.

data.title: Amid oil price panic, Carnival and Viking are called undervalued by Morgan Stanley
data.description:
Morgan Stanley upgraded Carnival (CCL) to an Overweight rating after having the cruise line stock slotted at Equal Weight.

Analyst Jamie Rollo and the analyst team lowered FY26 and FY26 estimates on Carnival (CCL) to factor in softer revenue yield and higher fuel costs. It was noted that the 28% year-to-date share price decline for CCL is in line with its Iraqi War, Russia-Ukraine and Arab Spring drops, and all those down periods were followed by material rebounds. "We also think both CCL (internal levers, strong FCF) and the industry (lower orderbook) are in better shape than in prior downturns. Upgrade to Overweight on attractive risk-reward balance," updated Rollo.

On a broad look, Carnival (CCL) was noted to be showing good progress on improving returns by shuffling ships between brands, exiting underperforming hardware, adding high-return private destinations, and reducing costs. Falling net debt and interest costs were also highlighted as being important EPS drivers.

In terms of the other cruise line stocks, Morgan Stanley kept an Equal Weight rating on Royal Caribbean (RCL) on a risk-reward seen as less compelling. The firm stayed Overweight on Viking Holdings (VIK) after the recent pullback, despite higher European exposure, given a longer booking curve, more resilient consumer base, less exposure to onboard oscillations, and lower fuel sensitivity. Meanwhile, Morgan Stanley stuck with an Equal Weight rating on Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings (NCLH), pointing to concerns over uncertainty with Great Stirrup Cay and a need to balance any cost-cutting actions with reinvestment.

All four cruise line stocks are down more than 15% over the last three weeks.

data.title: Alcoa plunges as LME aluminum futures post biggest drop since 2018
data.description:
Alcoa (AA) plummeted 11% in early trading Thursday while aluminum futures on the London Metal Exchange fell by the most since 2018, as rising worries about the global economic impact of the war in Iran caused broad losses across industrial metals markets.

LME benchmark three-month aluminum (LMAHDS03:COM) fell as much as 8% to $3,115/ton before bouncing to $3,214/ton, still wiping out gains that had piled up since the start of the Middle East war that had been fueled by risks to regional aluminum supplies.

Still, "while buyers across Asia, Europe, and the U.S. are scrambling to secure aluminum, stockpiles in China continue to build, tempering some of the bullish price pressures," ANZ analysts said in a note, adding that higher aluminum prices outside of China are incentivizing producers there to export more, which mitigates some of the global supply risks.

The Middle East accounts for 8%-9% of global aluminum output, but produces only 3% of global alumina and 1% of bauxite, leaving smelters highly dependent on seaborne imports, analysts say.

Among other potentially relevant stocks: Century Aluminum (CENX) down 8.2%, Constellium (CSTM) down 4.6%, Kaiser Aluminum (KALU) down 3.9%, Norsk Hydro (NHYDY) down 3.5%.

data.title: Federal Reserve formally proposes new bank capital, G-SIB rules
data.description:
The Federal Reserve proposed three new capital rules for banks, seeking to make the regulatory regime more efficient and keep U.S. banks competitive, Vice Chair for Supervision Michelle Bowman said on Thursday.

Two of the proposals apply to the U.S.'s largest banks — the Basel III framework and the Global Systemically Important Bank ((G-SIB)) surcharge. These "streamline the risk-based capital framework using a single set of calculations, improve alignment between requirements and risk, and revise the G-SIB surcharge to better capture the risks of our largest and most complex banks," she said.

The third proposal updates capital requirements for smaller and less complex banks to better align requirements with risks of traditional lending.

The central bank, in its role of supervising banks, worked with the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency and the Federal Deposit Insurance Corp. to rework the capital and G-SIB proposals.

"Together, these changes would strengthen our overall capital framework, which would remain robust under the new regime," Bowman said. The vice chair had been a vocal critic of an earlier proposal that would have significantly increased capital requirements for the country's largest banks, saying that they "over-calibrated" the rules when compared with regulations in other parts of the world.

In describing the latest proposal, she said, "This proposal makes targeted, reasonable changes to better calibrate requirements based on risk. These changes will continue to promote safety and soundness and U.S. financial stability."

Bowman had previewed the proposed rules last week. Today's official introduction of the new regimes didn't much help bank stocks. The KBW Nasdaq Bank Index (BKX) slipped 1.0% in late morning trading on Thursday as most of the U.S. megabanks traded in the red. JPMorgan Chase (JPM) fell 0.7%, Morgan Stanley (MS) slipped 0.7%, Bank of America (BAC) dipped 0.9%, Wells Fargo (WFC) dropped 1.3%, and Goldman Sachs (GS) was off 1.0%. Citigroup (C) edged up 0.2%.
